text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 22 September 2025 

Dr A. Rahman  
St. George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Neurology Outpatients, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr A. Rahman,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-02-1991, Hospital No: H472903 NHS No. 9938475621  
27 Briar Grove, Wimbledon, London SW19 3LX 

Diagnoses: 

Paroxysmal events consistent with a seizure disorder; prior abnormal events now in sustained remission. 

Current Medication: 

None (no regular antiseizure medication). 

Plan: 

- Continue current dietetic regimen under dietitian supervision.  
- Maintain seizure precautions as per DVLA guidance should symptoms recur; patient already aware.  
- No routine follow-up required; open access back to clinic if any breakthrough events, medication changes, or concerns.  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today for routine follow-up. He reports excellent overall wellbeing and no adverse neurological symptoms. He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years. He attributes much of the improvement to sustained adherence to dietetic guidance (a ketogenic-style plan devised with our specialist dietitian), improved sleep hygiene, regular physical activity, and consistent hydration. He has had no emergency attendances, no injuries, and no postictal phenomena for several years.

There is no current antiseizure therapy in place; he self-discontinued medication over three years ago in a stepwise manner after discussion with his previous clinician, and there has been no recurrence. He monitors for potential triggers (sleep deprivation, intercurrent illness, missed meals) and keeps a simple diary; there have been no entries for events. Neurological examination today is normal. Routine bloods through his GP earlier this year (including metabolic profile and vitamin D) were satisfactory per patient report; I have requested copies for our records.

Given the durable remission and good tolerability of the nutritional strategy, I have advised he continue the current plan with periodic review by the dietitian to ensure adequate micronutrient intake and to consider gradual liberalisation if desired. We discussed red flags and a pragmatic plan should any events recur, including prompt GP contact and re-referral to our service for reassessment and consideration of therapy. He is comfortable with an open follow-up arrangement rather than scheduled appointments.

With best wishes,

entities.text: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient is currently in long-term remission and has been seizure free for years, with no entries for events in his diary and no emergency attendances or postictal phenomena for several years. This satisfies the criterion of at least 6 months seizure-free; indeed, it's years. Past history mentions seizures 2 per year previously, but current status is seizure-free for years. Therefore, the correct normalized label is 'seizure free for multiple years'.
seizure_frequency_number: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years
reference: He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
